one_line_takeaway: Recasting green-list recovery as a minimal-green-set MIP over the watermark's own z-test detection constraints lets an attacker steal the list and strip low-key/Unigram LLM watermarks with high precision even with zero prior knowledge and heavily mislabeled data, undermining the claim that fewer-key watermarks are robust.
key_finding: The MIP attack succeeds across all settings and beats the frequency baseline on precision and removal. AS1: Oracle-AS1 precision frequently exceeds 90% and nears 100% (e.g., 99.20% at gamma=0.25,delta=2, 40k on LLaMA), Vanilla-AS1 typically ~70-90%, and Pro-AS1 approximates the oracle with only an 8.49% precision gap (vs 5.91% for Vanilla-vs-Oracle on LLaMA); at gamma=0.25,delta=2, size 40k, Vanilla/Oracle/Pro beat freq by 11.14/35.83/27.79 precision points. AS2 (no knowledge): precision improves over freq by 2.07%-27.87% on LLaMA and rises with dataset size (unlike freq, which stays flat); on removal, the AS2 attack strips on average 77.38% (OPT) and 81.83% (LLaMA) of green tokens vs 68.08% and 43.02% for freq, and across all 32 settings averages GRR 20.39% vs freq's 44.46%. The attack is robust to countermeasures: even with 70% erroneous samples (r_c=0.7) it still removes ~40% of green tokens and evades the detector on 81.55% of sentences, versus 0.12% for freq. Multi-key (3 keys): precision at least 23 points above freq, averaging 76.70%. Pro-AS1's Stage-1 bound estimate is close to ground truth (avg gap 5.18/3.23 for watermarked/natural on LLaMA vs 24.64/16.55 for the naive threshold g_i). Gumbel-Softmax lowers post-removal perplexity by 0.4368 (OPT) and 0.2603 (LLaMA) vs greedy, though both roughly double raw perplexity (~3 -> ~7-8).
core_contribution: The paper proposes the first systematic green-list-stealing / watermark-removal attack against state-of-the-art low-key LLM text watermarks (Unigram/single-key and few-key green-red-list schemes). Its own contribution is to formalize green-list recovery as a Mixed Integer Programming (MIP) problem whose objective is to find the SMALLEST set of green tokens (minimizing a frequency-ratio weighted sum of token color codes c_j) subject to constraints derived directly from the watermark's own z-test detection rule (green-token counts must exceed the threshold g_i in watermarked sentences and fall below it in natural ones). This is contrasted with, and shown to beat, the prior frequency-based stealing baseline of Zhao et al. [31], which the authors position as the only existing green-list stealing method and which they cite (Related Work), not claim. Concretely the paper contributes: (1) three AS1 variants -- Vanilla-AS1 (loose threshold constraint), Oracle-AS1 (assumes exact per-sentence green-token counts), and Pro-AS1 (a two-stage optimization that estimates tighter per-sentence bounds b_i to approximate the oracle); (2) an AS2 method for a no-knowledge attacker that introduces binary selector variables lambda_i to tolerate/exclude erroneous (mislabeled) samples; (3) a multi-key generalization using per-sentence key-assignment variables rho_i^k solved by an iterative fix-and-reoptimize algorithm (Algorithm 1); and (4) two downstream removal strategies -- greedy synonym substitution and a Gumbel-Softmax method that optimizes perplexity to preserve fluency.
threat_model: Attacker's goal is to remove an LLM watermark by reconstructing its green list and substituting green tokens with red synonyms. Two knowledge levels are defined. AS1 (Watermark Detector API): attacker has black-box LLM access, can query the online detector API to label sentences as watermarked/natural, and knows gamma (green-list proportion) and the z-test threshold z*. AS2 (No-Knowledge, the paper's headline 'extreme' setting): attacker has NO detector API access and does not know gamma or z*; it can only assume LLM-generated corpora are watermarked and wild text is natural, so its dataset contains erroneous (mislabeled) samples. In both settings the perturbation strength delta is NOT required, and model parameters are treated as a black box. The paper also stresses the attack targets the schemes previously argued to be MORE robust (fewer/single keys), arguing that this robustness is insufficient.
limitations: Evaluated only on two relatively small open models (OPT-1.3B, LLaMA-2-7B); no closed/large-model or real deployed-API validation. The attack targets green-red-list z-test watermarks (Unigram / few-key with keys <5, tested with up to 3 keys) and does not address distortion-free or cryptographic watermark families. It depends on a commercial MIP solver (Gurobi) and the authors themselves note the global optimum is impractical to reach in finite time on large datasets, leaving scalability/time cost only implicitly characterized. Effectiveness grows with the amount of collected watermarked+natural corpus, so the strongest results assume large datasets (up to 40k). The method deliberately optimizes precision over recall. Watermark removal degrades fluency (perplexity roughly doubles) even with Gumbel-Softmax. The multi-key formulation assumes exactly one key per sentence. Proposed defenses (adding synonyms to the green list; unbiased watermarks) are only discussed, not evaluated.

datasets_used: C4 dataset (prompts randomly sampled to query the LLM for watermarked text; equal number of natural texts form the paired datasets)
Watermark settings: (gamma=0.25, delta=2), (0.25, 4), (0.5, 2), (0.5, 4); detection threshold z*=4
quantitative_results: Oracle-AS1 precision reaches 99.20% (gamma=0.25,delta=2,40k,LLaMA) and frequently >90%, nearing 100%; Vanilla-AS1 typically ~70-90%
At gamma=0.25,delta=2,40k on LLaMA, precision gain over freq baseline: Vanilla +11.14, Oracle +35.83, Pro +27.79 points
Pro-AS1 approximates Oracle with an 8.49% precision gap (vs 5.91% Vanilla-vs-Oracle) on LLaMA
AS2 precision improvement over freq on LLaMA: 2.07%-27.87%, increasing with dataset size
AS2 removal: avg green tokens removed 77.38% (OPT) and 81.83% (LLaMA) vs freq 68.08% and 43.02%
Across all 32 settings (4 watermark x 4 sizes x 2 LLMs) average GRR: ours 20.39% vs freq 44.46%
AS1 removal avg reduction on LLaMA (Vanilla/Oracle/Pro): 26.36% / 49.75% / 29.99%; Oracle GRR as low as 3.46%
Robustness to erroneous samples: at r_c=0.7 still removes ~40% green tokens; detector evasion 81.55% of sentences vs freq 0.12%
Multi-key (3 keys): precision >=23 points above freq, average precision 76.70% (e.g., LLaMA gamma=0.5,12k: 91.34% vs 71.65%)
Bound estimation (LLaMA): Pro-AS1 avg |b_i - g^o_i| = 5.18 (watermarked) / 3.23 (natural) vs |g_i - g^o_i| = 24.64 / 16.55; AS2 gaps 7.31 / 3.83
Gumbel-Softmax lowers perplexity vs greedy by 0.4368 (OPT) and 0.2603 (LLaMA); post-removal PPL ~7-8 vs raw ~3
baselines_compared: Frequency-based green-list stealing (Zhao et al. [31]) -- described as the only prior green-list stealing method; token is green if its frequency is higher in watermarked than natural text (for multi-key, top-mu most frequent tokens)
Internal comparisons among the paper's own variants: Vanilla-AS1, Oracle-AS1, Pro-AS1, and AS2
Removal-strategy comparison: greedy synonym substitution vs Gumbel-Softmax perplexity optimization
